name: Mozambican Portuguese

ancestors.notes: Colonial and post-colonial European Portuguese standard. Mozambique was a Portuguese colony until independence in 1975; educated speech follows European norms more closely than Angola or Brazil.
Swahili substrate/adstrate: spoken across northern Mozambique (Cabo Delgado, Nampula) and as a trade language; some phonological patterns transferred.
Nyanja/Chewa substrate: major language of central Mozambique (Tete, Zambézia); five-vowel system transfers into Portuguese learner varieties.
Tsonga/Tswa-Ronga substrate: dominant in southern Mozambique (Gaza, Maputo provinces); influences tonality and vowel quality in Maputo Portuguese.

notes: Mozambican Portuguese (Português de Moçambique, PM): the emerging non-native/L2 variety described by Gonçalves as arising from acquisition of Portuguese as a second language by children with Bantu mother tongues (Génese do Português de Moçambique, 2010; ~6.5% L1, the majority learning a Bantu language first — Barbosa 2011:202-203, reviewing Gonçalves 2010:7,25). NOT a creole. Modelled on the prestige Maputo educated variety. GROUNDED FEATURES: (1) WEAKER UNSTRESSED VOWEL REDUCTION than EP — /ɨ/ largely absent, unstressed /e/ tends to stay [e] (emerging-norm characterisation in Gonçalves 2010, ch. 2 'propriedades fónicas'; the direct instrumental study read here is Undolo 2014 for Angola, so the PM vowel figures are modelled by analogy to the shared Lusophone-African pattern and flagged accordingly in docs/languages/pt-MZ.md). (2) RHOTIC (Nhatuve 2019:136-137, Maputo oral Portuguese, READ): the strong rhotic <r>/<rr> is realised as an alveolar multiple vibrant [r]~[R] in all contexts, not the EP uvular [ʁ], and the /R/–/ɾ/ contrast tends to neutralise ('desaparece o fonema /r/'), a calque of the Bantu changana/ronga system which has only [R]; word-final coda <r> often triggers paragoge with epenthetic [ɨ] ([falaɾ]→[falarɨ]). (3) SIBILANT: coda /s/ stays alveolar [s], not palatalised to [ʃ] (the alveolar coda is declared in positional_graphemes and the inherited EP chiado rules are overridden by id below). (4) <lh> /ʎ/ shows voiced aspiration in Maputo speech, a Bantu calque (Nhatuve 2019:137) — documented but NOT modelled (narrow allophonic detail). UNDER-SOURCED (kept from prior skeleton, flagged in the doc page): coda /l/ → [w] vocalisation and Bantu-substrate pitch effects are not confirmed by the primaries read here and should be treated as tentative.

sources.author: Gonçalves, P.
sources.title: Português de Moçambique: uma variedade em formação

sources.author: Stroud, C. & Gonçalves, P. (eds.)
sources.title: Panorama do Português Oral de Maputo
sources.publisher: Instituto Nacional do Desenvolvimento da Educação
sources.notes: 3-volume study of spoken Portuguese in Maputo

sources.author: Lopes, A.J.
sources.title: A batalha das línguas: perspectivas sobre linguística aplicada em Moçambique
sources.notes: Bibliographic reference (not read in full for this spec).

sources.author: Nhatuve, Diocleciano
sources.title: Para a normatização do português de Moçambique: aspectos fonético fonológicos da vibrante <r> e da lateral <lh> no português oral de Maputo
sources.notes: Read in full. p.136: in Maputo oral Portuguese the onset <r> is realised as an alveolar multiple vibrant [R] regardless of the surrounding vowel, and word-final coda <r> takes an epenthetic vowel by paragoge ([falarɨ]); the /r/–/R/ opposition tends to disappear. p.137: <lh> /ʎ/ surfaces with voiced aspiration, a calque of changana/ronga (Bantu) where all <h> graphemes are aspirated; in the north (Emakhuwa area), lacking /R/ and /r/, the rhotic desvibrates to velar [l]. Concludes the Maputo variety is characterised by [R] in all contexts and the aspirated lateral, diverging from both EP and BP.

sources.author: Gonçalves, Perpétua
sources.title: A Génese do Português de Moçambique
sources.publisher: Imprensa Nacional-Casa da Moeda, Lisboa (229 pp., ISBN 978-972-27-1882-0)
sources.notes: Foundational monograph on PM as an emerging non-native variety acquired as L2 by Bantu-L1 children (p.7); ch. 2 characterises the phonic, lexical and (morpho)syntactic properties of the educated PM subvariety (p.25). Accessed via the detailed review of Barbosa (2011) rather than the full monograph; page attributions for the phonic-properties claim are to that review's discussion of the book's ch. 2.

sources.author: Barbosa, Joaquim
sources.title: Recensão: Perpétua Gonçalves. A Génese do Português de Moçambique
sources.publisher: Revista de Estudos Linguísticos da Universidade do Porto 6(1), 201-204
sources.notes: Read in full. Review of Gonçalves (2010): PM emerges from L2 acquisition by children with Bantu L1s (p.202, only ~6.5% of Mozambicans have Portuguese as L1); ch. 2 of the monograph describes the phonic properties of the educated PM subvariety (p.203, citing Gonçalves 2010:25).

allophone_rules.notes: Mozambican Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /s/ (no Lisbon 'chiado'); this overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_S_HUSH rule by id, matching the alveolar coda already declared in this spec's positional_graphemes.
Mozambican Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /z/; overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_Z_HUSH rule by id.

DISABLED for this variety. The EP Lisbon-standard centralisation of the word-final ⟨-em⟩ nucleus to [ɐ̃] (inherited from pt-PT) does not apply here: this variety keeps the close-mid nasal nucleus [ẽ], so ⟨-em⟩ is [ẽj̃] (glide via PT_FINAL_EM_GLIDE), not [ɐ̃j̃]. Peripheral/non-Iberian Portuguese does not follow the Lisbon centralisation.
DISABLED for this variety: word-final ⟨-em⟩ stays a nasal MONOPHTHONG [ẽ] (no palatal offglide) here.

stress.notes: Acordo Ortográfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam). DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly — without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. ⟨oe⟩ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, §Ditongos).

location.notes: Maputo. A national L2 variety: the capital stands in for the country. Weak. [Wikidata Q3889 P625]
